content.full_text:
tate of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 1097 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

916 372 3650        322 [illegible]        322 6441
                                           322-6444

July 21, 1979

A. David Stutz
Attorney at Law
Post Office Box 1669
San Diego, CA 92103

Dear Mr. Stutz:

Thank you for your letter on behalf of Robert G.
Peterson dated May 30, 1979, regarding the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act. I hope
that the following informal advice may be helpful.

Your letter divided your inquiry into subparts A
and B. With respect to part A, the facts as we understand them are as follows. Mr. Peterson would like to
enter into a lease or other business relationship with
the San Diego Port District for the purpose of renovating
a wharf to create shops and possibly restaurants. The
Port Commission must approve the lease or contract. The
Commission is a seven member board, three of whom are
appointed by the San Diego City Council. The question
presented for our consideration is whether Mr. Peterson's
spouse, Maureen O'Connor, who serves as a Councilmember,
is disqualified from participating in the Council's
selection of Port Commissioners.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that:

No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.

Government Code Section 87103 points out that:

An official has a financial interest in
a decision within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the

A. David Utter
July 21, 1978
Page Two

decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, can:

(a) Any business entity in which the
public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the
public official had a direct or indirect
interest worth more than one thousand dol-
lars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than
loans in a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child
of a public official....

Therefore, in the instant case, Ms. O'Connor would
be disqualified from making, participating in and making
or using her official position to influence any govern-
mental decision which will foreseeably and materially
affect her husband's business interests. In order for a
material financial effect to be "foreseeable" within the
meaning of the Act, there must be a reasonable likelihood
that the contemplated effect will occur. A mere showing
that the effect could conceivably occur is not sufficient
to establish foreseeability. Based on the facts pro-
vided for our consideration, it is not foreseeable that
her participation in a vote to select a port commissioner
will affect her financial interest. In order for
Ms. O'Connor to be disqualified, it must be foreseeable
that her vote on the Council will lead to the selection
of a port commissioner who would cast his or her vote

A. David Schulz
July 21, 1978
Page Three

in a fashion favorable to Ms. O'Connor's spouse, in a
situation in which the port commissioner's veto would
determine the result of a Port Commission decision
concerning Ms. O'Connor's spouse's proposals. In the
absence of special circumstances, we believe that the
probability that such a chain of events will occur is
not so likely as to be foreseeable within the meaning
of the Act. Accordingly, Ms. O'Connor is not prohibited
from participating in the decision regarding the appointment
of port commissioners.

Part B of your letter states that Councilmember
Maureen O'Connor's spouse is forming a partnership with
Olaf Tron. Mr. Tron is seeking a lease/concession with
the City for a train station renovation project which
must be approved by the Council. It is our understanding that the project is directed to the partnership.
More specifically, monies expended or collected by
Mr. Tron in connection with the project, as well as
the profits or losses, will go in any way be commingled
with the assets of the partnership.

The question you have asked us to consider is
whether Councilmember O'Connor may vote on the matter
regarding the lease. As previously mentioned above,
the Political Reform Act provides that Councilmember
O'Connor cannot vote on matters where she or her spouse
has a financial interest.

Because her spouse has a financial interest in
the partnership, Councilmember O'Connor may not vote
on matters which might materially affect the partnership. If Mr. Tron's proposed project could materially
benefit the partnership in any way, Councilmember
O'Connor may not vote on matters that might affect the
proposed project.

For example, if funds from the proposed project
will be commingled with the partnership or if the
partnership will foreseeably benefit from increased
business due to the project, Ms. O'Connor would be
disqualified from participating in decision regarding
the project. She would also be disqualified if an
arrangement will be created between Mr. Tron,
Mr. Derbowski, Ms. O'Connor and/or the partnership for
the purpose of providing additional compensation,
partnership shares or future business opportunities
to either Ms. O'Connor or her spouse based upon the

A. David Stein
July 21, 1979
Page Four


success of the project. In other words, none of the
benefits generated by the success of Mr. Tron's project
may be permitted to directly or indirectly benefit
Ms. O'Connor or her spouse. If, at the time of
Ms. O'Connor's decision on the project, it is fore-
seeable that such benefits would flow to Ms. O'Connor
or Mr. Peterson, her participation in that decision
will be in violation of the Act.

Lastly, Section 37103(c) provides that a public
official may not participate in a decision which will
foreseeably and materially affect a source of income
to the official if the official has received $250 or
more in income within the twelve months prior to the
decision in question. Therefore, if Councilmember
O'Connor has received $250 or more, either directly
from Mr. Tron or through her community or society share
of her husband's income from Mr. Tron, within the
twelve months prior to consideration of the project,
the disqualification provisions of the Act will apply.

If I may be of further assurance, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Dwight M. Dickerson
Local Counsel
Conflict of Interest Division

DED:DJE

A. DAVID STUTZ
ATTORNEY AT LAW
2170 4th AVE.
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
236-1133

May 30, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Advisory Opinion

Dear Sir:

On behalf of a client I hereby request an advisory opinion as to whether a conflict of interest exists pursuant to the Political Reform Act of 1974, under the following facts:

a. Businessman A wants to enter into a lease or other business relationship with a Port District. The City Council of City X appoints as a body three (3) members to the seven (7) member Port Commission. Businessman A is married to a member of the City Council. The Port Commission must approve the lease or contract.

Question presented: Is there a Conflict of Interest by the spouse member of the City Council?

b. Businessman A is a partner with Businessman B in unrelated transactions. Business B, alone, wants to enter into a lease/contract with City X. Businessman A is married to a member of the City Council of City X, which must approve the lease/contract with Businessman B.

Question presented: Is there a Conflict of Interest by the spouse member of the City Council?

Thank you in advance for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

A. David Stutz
Attorney at Law

ADS:tlr

Thank you in advance for your cooperation.
